methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $41M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 25.8x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
Dragonfly Aerospace is a Series G-stage space and aerospace company founded by Bryan Dean that has raised $20M to date. The valuation exercise draws on a peer universe of 13 comparable companies across the space and aerospace sector—5 public entities (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and 8 private firms including SpaceX, Sierra Space, and Planet Labs—though stage-specific peer density remains limited.

Stage-progression analysis is the primary applicable method here. Using total capital raised ($20M) as a proxy for enterprise value, we apply a 1.25x step-up multiplier typical for Series G advancement, yielding an implied valuation of $51M. Public-market anchoring via the sector's median P/S multiple of 25.8x cannot be applied without disclosed revenue figures, which are unavailable for private companies. Peer-median comparison similarly lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage datapoints—only 0 narrow-set matches exist within the Space & Aerospace sector—making cross-company multiples unreliable.

The triangulated valuation range is $44M to $59M, with a median of $51M. This estimate carries material uncertainty: it relies on a single methodology and lacks revenue-based validation. Key unknowns include the company's current revenue run rate and capital deployment efficiency relative to seed-stage peers. Without additional operational metrics, the $51M midpoint should be treated as a preliminary anchor rather than a deterministic fair value.
